A History of Mind

Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern

Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

Henri Bergson

1859 – 1941

Bergson’s philosophy of mind can be extracted as a temporal, action-oriented spiritual realism.

Bergson’s philosophy of mind can be extracted as a temporal, action-oriented spiritual realism. Its central claim is that mind is not primarily a thing, a brain-state, a representation, or a container of ideas. Mind is duration: a living continuity in which the past is preserved, the future is anticipated, and novelty is inserted into the world through action. The body and brain do not manufacture consciousness; they select, canalize, inhibit, and actualize it for practical life. The soul, in its strongest Bergsonian sense, is the whole personal duration considered as memory, freedom, creative self-expression, and, at its highest intensity, openness to divine creative love.

This means Bergson is neither a Cartesian dualist in the usual sense, nor a materialist, nor an idealist. He is “frankly dualistic” in Matter and Memory, but his dualism is not a crude separation between a ghostly inner substance and an extended outer substance. It is a temporal dualism: matter tends toward present repetition, spatial exteriority, and necessity; spirit tends toward memory, contraction of the past, creative action, and freedom. The relation between body and soul is therefore not solved by locating mind “inside” the brain, but by asking how different rhythms of duration intersect in perception, memory, and action. Bergson himself says Matter and Memory affirms the reality of both spirit and matter, and tries to determine their relation through memory.

A good compressed formula would be this: the brain is the organ of attention to life; consciousness is the conservation of the past and anticipation of the future; the soul is the whole duration of a person pressing toward action, freedom, and, potentially, mystical love.

1. Terms: “mind,” “soul,” “spirit,” and “consciousness”

Bergson’s vocabulary is delicate. In French, conscience means consciousness, but can also carry moral resonance. Esprit can mean mind or spirit. Âme means soul, but in Bergson it should not be reduced either to ecclesiastical doctrine or to a detachable Cartesian substance. He uses these terms according to context. In psychological contexts, the soul is the inward continuity of personal life. In metaphysical contexts, spirit is the principle of memory, contraction, freedom, and creative action. In theological contexts, soul becomes the being capable of opening beyond closed social life toward divine love.

This terminological flexibility is important because Bergson’s thought moves across psychology, metaphysics, biology, ethics, and religion. He does not treat theology as an ornamental appendix. His last major work, The Two Sources of Morality and Religion, extends the same underlying theory of consciousness into social, moral, and mystical life. Static religion corresponds to the mind’s need for social stabilization and protection against fear; dynamic religion corresponds to a more intense creative movement of the soul.

Biographically, it is also important not to assimilate Bergson too quickly into a single confessional category. He was born into a Jewish family, wrote with extraordinary sympathy about Christian mysticism, and late in life refused exemption from Vichy’s anti-Jewish registration despite his attraction to Catholicism. He did not formally convert, though a priest attended his funeral.

2. Method: why Bergson thinks ordinary theories of mind go wrong

Bergson’s philosophy of mind begins with a methodological accusation: most theories falsify consciousness by spatializing time. They treat mental life as if it were a sequence of discrete, countable, externally related states, like beads on a string or points on a line. But consciousness as lived is not like that. Its moments interpenetrate. A feeling changes as it endures; an emotion deepens by taking up the whole history of the person; a decision is not the mechanical resultant of separable motives, but an act in which a whole self may be expressed.

This is why Bergson’s first major work, Time and Free Will, insists that pure duration cannot be represented as a line without distortion. Once we imagine conscious states as points placed side by side, we have already substituted space for time. In immediate consciousness, psychic states “melt” into one another, forming a qualitative multiplicity rather than a numerical multiplicity.

The consequence is radical. Bergson does not merely say that we have overlooked some subjective qualia. He says that much philosophical psychology begins from the wrong ontology. It starts from static states, then asks how they connect. Bergson starts from continuity, then asks how practical life cuts it into states, objects, words, and choices. Language is necessary, but language freezes movement. It fixes impersonal aspects of inner life and tends to make us confuse the symbol with the reality symbolized.

His method of “intuition” is not a vague hunch. It is a disciplined attempt to enter into duration rather than reconstruct it from spatial symbols. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy summarizes Bergsonian intuition as a method that divides mixed phenomena into differences in kind, enters into duration, and shows how apparent dualities such as matter and spirit are “sewn together” through memory.

3. Duration: consciousness as qualitative multiplicity

The first foundation of Bergson’s philosophy of mind is the concept of duration (durée). Duration is not clock time. Clock time is homogeneous, measurable, divisible, and spatialized. Duration is the lived continuity of becoming. It is heterogeneous, qualitative, cumulative, and irreversible.

When one listens to a melody, one does not first hear isolated notes and then synthesize them by an external operation. The earlier notes remain in the later ones as a felt continuity. The melody is not a sum of points; it is an unfolding. Bergson’s famous analyses of clock strokes and inner states aim to show that lived time is a qualitative organization, not a numerical collection.

This has several implications for mind.

First, a mental state is not an atom. A sorrow, desire, hope, or intention is not simply located at an instant. It is what it is by its place in the whole duration of the person. Bergson says deep states of consciousness reflect the whole personality, and that a free act is one that expresses the whole self.

Second, consciousness is internally historical. The past does not vanish; it is carried forward. Even when it is not explicitly recalled, it colors the present. The person is not a substance that has experiences as external additions; the person is the cumulative continuity of those experiences.

Third, prediction in the psychological domain is not analogous to prediction in mechanics. To foresee a future conscious act as if it were already given would be to confuse real duration with the spatialized time of science. Bergson’s critique of determinism is not that motives are absent, but that motives are not fixed external objects that combine mechanically. They become what they are within the developing person.

Bergson’s philosophy of mind is therefore anti-atomistic. It rejects the model in which the mind is composed of discrete mental contents externally related to one another. Mental life is a moving whole.

4. The self: surface self, deep self, and freedom

Bergson distinguishes between a superficial self and a deeper self. The superficial self is the self adapted to social life, language, practical action, and external measurement. It uses ready-made concepts and social categories. It is the self that says “I am angry,” “I prefer this,” “I choose that,” as though the inner life were a set of named, stable states.

The deep self is the person as duration. It is not hidden in a mysterious chamber; rather, it is the actual continuity of one’s history, affective tonality, memory, tendencies, and creative movement. The deep self is what is lost when consciousness is reduced to external signs or linguistic labels.

This distinction is crucial for Bergson’s theory of freedom. Freedom is not, for him, an arbitrary faculty of choosing between indifferent alternatives. Nor is it best understood through the abstract question, “Could I have done otherwise?” That question often imagines time as a spatial path with branching routes, as though the self could be placed again at a point already passed and made to choose a different path. Bergson thinks this picture falsifies becoming.

A free act is one in which the whole self expresses itself. Freedom comes in degrees, because acts can be more or less expressive of the deep self. Many actions are habitual, automatic, socially scripted, or externally suggested. A genuinely free act is rare because it requires that the person’s whole duration gather itself into action. Bergson’s language is not voluntarist in the simplistic sense; he does not mean that freedom is mere willpower. Freedom is the creative expression of a self that has become sufficiently integrated to act from its own duration.

This gives us a Bergsonian account of agency: an act is free when it is not merely the mechanical result of external causes or superficial motives, but the outward movement of a whole personal history. Freedom is not outside causality; it is a different kind of causality, the causality of duration expressing itself.

5. Matter as “images”: escaping both realism and idealism

In Matter and Memory, Bergson introduces one of his most unusual technical terms: image. Matter is an aggregate of images. By “image,” he does not mean a private mental representation. He means something “more than” what idealists call representation but “less than” what realists call a thing existing wholly in itself. This vocabulary is designed to bypass the sterile opposition between realism and idealism.

The body is one image among other images, but it is a privileged image because it is known not only from without but also from within, through affection. My body is not the container of the world. Rather, it is a center of action within the world of images. Bergson explicitly rejects the idea that the material world is inside the brain; the brain is itself part of the material world.

This is decisive for his philosophy of perception. Perception is not primarily the construction of an inner picture by the brain. Perception is oriented toward action. Objects appear to me in terms of my body’s possible actions upon them. The world is not fabricated by the nervous system, but practical perception selects from the world according to the needs and powers of the body.

Here Bergson anticipates, in a metaphysical register, later action-oriented and ecological approaches to perception. The perceived world is not a neutral inventory of properties, nor a subjective projection. It is a field of possible action.

6. Pure perception and concrete perception

Bergson distinguishes pure perception from concrete perception. Pure perception is a limiting concept: perception stripped of memory. It would be the body’s immediate relation to surrounding images in terms of possible action. In actual human life, however, perception is never purely present. It is always saturated by memory.

This distinction lets Bergson solve a problem that troubles both empiricism and representationalism. If perception were merely an internal copy, then one would have to explain how the mind escapes its representations to reach the world. Bergson avoids this by beginning from the world of images itself. Perception is already outside, among things, as a practical selection from them. The mental element enters not by fabricating the world, but by inserting memory into perception.

Concrete perception is therefore perception plus memory. When I see an object, I do not receive a bare sensory datum and then add an interpretation. My past descends into the present perception, enriching it, identifying it, orienting action toward it. This is why perception is always thicker than a sensory input. It carries habit, recognition, expectation, and personal history.

The body’s function is selective. Bergson says the body is an instrument of action and not a producer of representation; the brain explains action begun, prepared, or suggested, but not perception as such.

7. The brain: not producer of mind, but organ of action

Bergson’s most famous and controversial thesis is that the brain does not produce consciousness. More precisely, he denies that the brain stores the whole of memory, generates the whole of thought, or contains consciousness as an internal object. The brain is an organ of action, selection, and attention to life.

He repeatedly argues that the psychical state overflows the cerebral state. The brain indicates the part of mental life that is translatable into movement. A perfect observer of the brain, he suggests, would see gestures, attitudes, and nascent actions, but not the full inner life of thought and feeling.

In Mind-Energy, Bergson develops the point with particular force. From a mental state one might infer the corresponding cerebral attitude, because the mental state prepares possible action. But from the cerebral state alone one could not reconstruct the whole mental state, since the same cerebral configuration could be compatible with many different inner meanings.

This is not a denial of brain-mind correlation. Bergson accepts that the brain is intimately connected with mental life. His claim is about the interpretation of that correlation. Psychophysical parallelism may be a useful research principle, but Bergson rejects it when turned into a metaphysical dogma that makes mental life simply duplicate cerebral processes.

The brain, then, is a filter, relay, selector, and motor coordinator. It narrows consciousness to the requirements of present action. This narrowing is not a defect; it is what makes practical life possible. Without it, consciousness would be overwhelmed by the whole past and by countless irrelevant possibilities.

8. Attention to life

One of Bergson’s most important phrases is attention to life. It means the degree to which consciousness is tightened toward present practical demands. A healthy waking consciousness is not simply more conscious in every respect than a dreamer or contemplative. It is more effectively oriented toward action.

Bergson describes different “tones” or “heights” of mental life. Consciousness can contract toward the present and action, or dilate toward memory, dream, contemplation, and less immediately useful forms of awareness. Disturbances of mental life may occur when the tie between memory and motor adaptation is loosened; the person may lose practical grip not because the past has disappeared, but because it can no longer be appropriately actualized into action.

This is essential for understanding his account of pathology. Bergson does not usually interpret disorders as destruction of mind itself. He interprets them as disorders of actualization, attention, selection, motor schema, or adaptation. This is especially visible in his treatment of aphasia and memory loss.

9. Memory: habit memory and pure memory

Memory is the central hinge of Bergson’s philosophy of mind. He distinguishes two kinds of memory.

The first is habit memory. This is memory embodied in motor mechanisms. Learning a poem by heart, riding a bicycle, typing, playing an instrument, or automatically responding to a familiar situation are examples. Habit memory is practical, bodily, impersonal, and action-oriented. It is less a representation of the past than a capacity for present performance.

The second is pure memory or spontaneous recollection. This is memory of the past as past: dated, personal, singular, and preserved in its own tone. Bergson says spontaneous recollection is “perfect” from the outset in the sense that it is not built by repetition as habit is; repetition creates bodily habit, whereas pure memory conserves the unique event.

This distinction lets Bergson make a bold claim: the past does not cease to exist merely because it is no longer useful. It survives virtually. The mistake is to imagine memory as if it had to be stored in a spatial container, such as a brain-cell or cerebral warehouse. That model, Bergson argues, transfers the logic of space into duration.

The word “virtual” is crucial. Pure memory is not actual in the way a present perception is actual. But it is not unreal. It exists virtually as part of the person’s duration and becomes actual when it descends into perception, image, speech, or action. Bergson describes recollection as a movement from the past toward the present: we place ourselves in the past “at a stroke,” then actualize memory through successive planes until it becomes useful in the present.

In modern terms, Bergson’s theory should not be read as a simple empirical denial that the brain participates in memory. That would be untenable today. But his deeper point remains philosophically powerful: the lived past as personal meaning is not equivalent to a local storage item. A neural trace, even if indispensable, is not the whole reality of memory as dated, meaningful, personal duration.

10. The memory cone and the levels of mental life

Bergson’s famous “memory cone” is a model of how the past relates to present action. At the base of the cone lies the whole preserved past, virtual and unconscious. At the summit is the body, inserted into the present, oriented toward action. Between them are different planes of memory, from dreamlike expansion to practical contraction.

The model shows why consciousness is not all-or-nothing. The mind may operate at different levels of tension. At one extreme, memory is relaxed, expansive, contemplative, perhaps dreamlike. At the other extreme, it is narrowed toward bodily action. Normal waking life involves selecting from the past only what helps present action.

The Stanford Encyclopedia’s summary is helpful: habit memory concerns acquired motor patterns, while pure memory concerns the survival of personal memories, largely unconscious until called upon; in the cone model, memories descend from the virtual past toward present action.

This gives Bergson a theory of the unconscious, but not a Freudian one. The unconscious is not primarily a place of repressed wishes. It is the virtual survival of the past. Most of who we are is not explicitly present, yet it remains real and can be actualized under certain conditions.

11. Consciousness as memory plus anticipation

Bergson’s mature definition of consciousness is striking: consciousness means memory, but memory already implies anticipation. A being conscious of the present must retain what has just passed and lean toward what is about to happen. A mathematical instant has no consciousness. The lived present is a thickness, a bridge between immediate past and imminent future.

This makes consciousness intrinsically temporal. It is not a spotlight illuminating an instant. It is a contraction of duration. It holds the past and prepares the future.

Bergson links this to choice. A being that must choose must remember and foresee. Where action is automatic, consciousness can diminish. Where there is hesitation, indetermination, and possible novelty, consciousness intensifies. In Creative Evolution, he says consciousness corresponds to a living being’s power of choice and is coextensive with the fringe of possible action.

This gives us one of the most important theses for a Bergsonian philosophy of mind: consciousness is not added to action from outside; it appears where action is not mechanically predetermined. Consciousness is the internal light of possible action.

12. Life, consciousness, and freedom

Bergson extends this view biologically. Life is a movement that inserts indetermination into matter. Matter tends toward necessity, repetition, and spatialized exteriority. Life introduces delay, choice, invention, and freedom. Consciousness is therefore not an accidental ornament of life; it is life’s tendency toward freedom becoming explicit.

He is careful, however, not to say that every living being is conscious in the same way. Consciousness varies in degree and can be dormant. Plants, animals, and humans participate differently in life’s movement. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy summarizes this by noting Bergson’s view that consciousness appears in degrees and is linked to a faculty of action, while life and matter can be understood as two rhythms.

In Mind-Energy, Bergson formulates the point broadly: consciousness is coextensive with life “in right,” even if not always awake “in fact”; matter is necessity, consciousness is freedom, and life is freedom inserting itself into necessity.

This is not straightforward panpsychism. Bergson does not simply attribute consciousness to every bit of matter. Rather, consciousness belongs to the line of life insofar as life delays automatic reaction and opens a field of possible action. Consciousness may shrink, sleep, or become nearly null where choice is minimal.

13. Intelligence, instinct, and intuition

Bergson’s philosophy of mind also includes a theory of cognition. He distinguishes intelligence, instinct, and intuition.

Intelligence is adapted to matter, manipulation, tools, stable objects, and spatial relations. It is powerful because it decomposes, measures, compares, and reconstructs. But precisely because it is practical and spatializing, it has difficulty grasping duration, life, and creation. It treats the moving as if it were made of immobilities and the living as if it were a rearrangement of the already given.

Instinct is more immediately attuned to life, but it remains practical and usually non-reflective. It knows how to act without conceptually knowing that it knows.

Intuition is instinct become disinterested, reflective, and enlarged. It is not irrationality. It is a disciplined sympathy with duration. It tries to coincide with the movement of the real rather than reconstruct that movement from static concepts. Bergson describes intuition as a way for philosophy to re-enter the creative impulse that intelligence spatializes.

This matters for philosophy of mind because Bergson thinks the mind cannot be adequately understood by the very faculty whose natural function is to manipulate external objects. Intelligence is indispensable, including in science. But when intelligence becomes metaphysics, it tends to mistake its practical symbols for the structure of reality. Philosophy must therefore use intelligence against its own distortions, then return to intuition.

14. Thought, effort, and the dynamic schema

Bergson’s theory of cognition is not limited to perception and memory. In his essay on intellectual effort, he argues that thinking often begins not with a ready-made image but with a dynamic schema: a vague, virtual, directional whole that seeks realization in images, words, or actions. Intellectual effort is the passage from a less realized to a more realized state, from intensive implication to extensive articulation.

This gives a sophisticated account of creativity and problem-solving. A thought is not always assembled from discrete representations. Often we first possess a felt direction, a virtual organization, or an implicit whole. The work of thinking consists in finding the images, words, or operations that actualize it.

This fits the broader metaphysics: mind is not a container of representations but a movement of actualization. The virtual becomes actual through effort.

15. Body and soul: overflow, dependence, and independence

Bergson’s mature position on soul and body is subtle. He does not say the soul is simply independent of the body in every respect. The body is indispensable for action, perception, orientation, social communication, and practical life. But he insists that the soul overflows the body in both space and time.

In Mind-Energy, especially the lecture “The Soul and the Body,” Bergson says he is not trying to define the hidden essence of soul and body, but to determine what experience shows about their union and difference. The “I” overflows the body because it reaches beyond the body in perception and because it carries a past irreducible to the present organism.

He also says the mind draws from itself more than it contains in a static sense: it creates acts by creating itself anew. This is a crucial phrase for Bergson’s philosophy of mind. The mind is not a fixed substance with fixed predicates. It is a creative continuity capable of becoming more than it was.

The brain, then, is not nothing. It is the point where spirit inserts itself into matter. It is the cutting edge of action. But the whole of spiritual life is not contained in it.

16. Aphasia, memory disorders, and the anti-localization argument

Bergson’s treatment of aphasia and memory disorders is central to his case against cerebral storage theories. He argues that lesions do not prove that memories are stored in the damaged tissue. They may instead damage the mechanisms by which memories are evoked, selected, expressed, or translated into action.

In his analysis, the cerebral organ prepares the motor frame but does not furnish the recollection itself. He compares the brain to an organ of “pantomime”: it expresses, prepares, and coordinates action, but it is not the complete source of inner meaning.

This argument must be handled carefully. As an empirical claim about neurology, parts of Bergson’s discussion are dated. Contemporary neuroscience gives the brain a much more detailed role in memory formation, consolidation, retrieval, and impairment than Bergson could acknowledge. But as a philosophical challenge, his argument remains significant: the correlation between memory and brain processes does not by itself settle the ontological status of memory as lived, dated, personal past.

A charitable contemporary reconstruction would say: neural systems are conditions of memory’s actualization and expression, but the meaning of memory as personal duration is not exhausted by a spatially locatable trace.

17. Dreams, abnormal perception, and psychical research

Bergson’s interest in dreams, telepathy, and psychical research can look eccentric today, but it is not incidental. It follows from his general thesis that the body filters consciousness for practical life. If ordinary perception is selective rather than exhaustive, then unusual states might reveal aspects of mental life normally suppressed by practical adaptation.

In Mind-Energy, Bergson treats sensory and cerebral organs as instruments of selection. They shut out what is not practically useful. His discussion of “phantasms” and psychical research arises from the possibility that, under exceptional conditions, this practical filtering might be loosened.

This does not mean one must accept Bergson’s openness to psychical phenomena. His empirical evidence is historically dated. But philosophically, this theme clarifies the structure of his theory: normal consciousness is not maximal consciousness; it is practically filtered consciousness. The waking, socially competent person is not necessarily the being most open to the full range of mind.

18. Survival after death

Bergson’s view of survival is cautious but real. He does not present a simple dogmatic proof of immortality. Rather, he argues that if mental life is much vaster than cerebral life, and if the brain functions mainly as a selector and action-organ, then the disappearance of the body does not automatically entail the annihilation of consciousness. He even suggests that survival becomes probable and that the burden of proof shifts toward the denier.

This must be understood within his system. If the soul were merely a function produced by the brain, death would end it. If, however, the brain is the organ that limits and actualizes a wider spiritual life for action, then death may be the loss of a practical instrument rather than the destruction of the whole soul.

Still, Bergson leaves open major questions. What form would survival take without the body’s action-orientation? Would personal memory remain organized? Would individuality persist? Would consciousness expand, dissolve, or transform? His philosophy makes survival metaphysically intelligible; it does not supply a detailed eschatology.

19. Theological extension: why religion is not a distraction

The theological dimension is not secondary. It reveals what Bergson thinks consciousness can become at its highest intensity.

In The Two Sources of Morality and Religion, Bergson distinguishes closed and open forms of moral-religious life. Closed morality belongs to the cohesive pressure of society. It binds individuals to the group. Static religion supports this closed order by producing representations that counteract fear, egoism, social dissolution, and the demoralizing effects of intelligence.

Open morality, by contrast, comes from aspiration, creative emotion, and exemplary souls. It does not merely enforce social obligation; it draws humanity forward. Bergson associates this with saints, heroes, and mystics whose lives communicate an emotion capable of opening other souls.

This is a theory of mind as well as religion. It says the soul has two social modes. In the closed mode, consciousness contracts around group survival, habit, obligation, and defensive representation. In the open mode, consciousness expands beyond the closed group through creative love. Theological language names this expansion not as an abstract ideal but as contact with the creative source of life.

20. Static religion and fabulation

Static religion is produced by what Bergson calls the fabulatory function. Human intelligence is powerful, but it also creates dangers. It lets us foresee death, calculate self-interest against the group, hesitate, and become demoralized. Static religion compensates by generating images, narratives, prohibitions, and assurances that protect social cohesion and practical vitality.

This is not simply a debunking of religion. Bergson does not say static religion is “false” in the crude sense of being useless illusion. He treats it as a natural function of life. It serves survival. It is a psychological and social mechanism by which life repairs some of the risks introduced by intelligence.

But static religion remains closed. It supports the group, not humanity as such. It protects life, but it does not reach the deepest creativity of spiritual life.

21. Dynamic religion and mysticism

Dynamic religion is different in kind. It is not primarily a system of representations, sanctions, or dogmas. It is rooted in mystical experience and creative emotion. Bergson says genuine mysticism is not mere contemplation, vision, or ecstasy. The true mystic is a person of action. The mystical soul receives a current and spreads it as love, marked by personality and creative power.

This point is central. Bergson does not define higher consciousness as escape from the world. The mystic does not merely see; the mystic acts. Contemplation is incomplete unless it becomes transformative action. In this respect, the mystical life is the highest form of the same structure seen throughout Bergson’s philosophy: duration becomes real by actualizing itself creatively.

Bergson also rejects the easy pathologizing of mysticism. He acknowledges visions and ecstasies, and admits that some phenomena may be hallucinatory or linked with nervous disturbance. But he argues that the great mystics show robust practical health: action, adaptation, discernment, perseverance, and good sense.

22. Christian mysticism, Christ, and the open soul

Bergson treats Christian mysticism as the most complete historical form of dynamic religion. His reason is not merely doctrinal preference. It is structural: Christian mysticism, as he interprets it, passes beyond contemplation into action and universal love. He sees the great Christian mystics as continuators or imitators of Christ, while also treating each as original and incomplete.

Spanish-language scholarship has emphasized this point strongly. One UNED study presents Bergson as giving Christian mysticism philosophical value in relation to metaphysical questions about God and the soul, and as treating mysticism not only as the culmination of religion but as its root.

This matters for philosophy of mind because the “open soul” is not just morally nicer than the closed soul. It is a different regime of consciousness. It is consciousness released from the exclusive pressure of utility, group identity, fear, and repetition. It is not disembodied; it acts. But it acts from a deeper source.

23. God as creative love

In Creative Evolution, Bergson speaks of God not as a finished thing but as unceasing life, action, and freedom: a continuity of creative impulse rather than a static being containing all possibilities in advance.

In The Two Sources, the formulation becomes more explicitly theological and affective: God is love and the object of love. Divine love is not merely one attribute among others; Bergson says, in effect, that divine love is God. He connects this with the idea that creation is an enterprise of making creators.

This is not classical theism without remainder. Bergson’s God is not primarily the unmoved mover of scholastic metaphysics. Nor is Bergson simply giving us a psychological projection. God is the creative source disclosed most directly in mystical action. The divine is known not by detached speculation alone, but by participation in the creative movement of love.

For philosophy of mind, this means that the highest form of consciousness is not theoretical representation. It is creative participation. The soul reaches its highest intensity when it joins the movement of love that creates new forms of life.

24. “Mechanics requires mysticism”: technology and the enlarged body

One of Bergson’s most prophetic theological-anthropological claims appears near the end of The Two Sources. Modern machinery has enlarged the human body. Technology extends our powers enormously. But the soul has not grown proportionally. Humanity now possesses an enlarged body without a sufficient enlargement of soul. Bergson writes that this enlarged body awaits a “supplement of soul,” and that mechanics requires mysticism.

This is not a sentimental anti-technology claim. It follows from his philosophy of mind. Technical intelligence extends action but does not by itself deepen consciousness. Intelligence can manipulate matter, build machines, and coordinate vast systems, but without mystical or moral enlargement it may intensify closed egoism, domination, and fear.

The mind-body problem here becomes civilizational. Humanity has extended its bodily powers through machines; it must now expand the soul capable of directing those powers.

25. Extracting a comprehensive Bergsonian philosophy of mind

A full Bergsonian philosophy of mind can be stated in the following theses.

First, mind is duration. The basic reality of consciousness is not a collection of states but a qualitative continuity in which moments interpenetrate.

Second, mental life is historical through and through. The past survives virtually and colors the present. A person is not a bare subject plus experiences; the person is the continuing organization of those experiences.

Third, perception is action-oriented selection. The body does not create the world as an internal representation. It selects from the field of images according to possible action.

Fourth, the brain is an organ of insertion into action. It selects, inhibits, coordinates, and actualizes consciousness for practical life. It does not contain the whole of mind.

Fifth, memory has two poles: habit memory and pure memory. Habit memory is bodily, motor, and practical. Pure memory is personal, dated, virtual, and spiritual.

Sixth, consciousness is memory plus anticipation. The present is not an instant but a thickness joining immediate past to imminent future.

Seventh, consciousness varies with choice. Where action is automatic, consciousness contracts or sleeps. Where indetermination and possible novelty appear, consciousness intensifies.

Eighth, freedom is self-expression. A free act is not arbitrary choice but an act expressing the whole personal duration.

Ninth, intelligence is practical and spatializing. It is indispensable for action and science, but it falsifies duration when it claims metaphysical sovereignty.

Tenth, intuition is disciplined access to duration. It is not anti-intellectual, but it resists the reduction of life and mind to static conceptual reconstruction.

Eleventh, the soul overflows the body. It depends on the body for action but is not exhausted by bodily mechanisms.

Twelfth, the highest form of consciousness is open, creative, and loving. Mysticism is not an accidental religious curiosity; it is Bergson’s name for the soul participating most fully in the creative movement of life.

Thirteenth, theology and philosophy of mind converge in the idea of creative love. God is not an inert object of theory but the creative source with which the open soul comes into contact through action.

26. Relation to contemporary philosophy of mind

Bergson’s philosophy can be brought into conversation with several contemporary positions, though it cannot be identified simply with any of them.

It resembles embodied and enactive theories because perception is tied to possible action, and the brain is treated as part of an organism-world circuit rather than as a private theater of representations.

It resembles anti-reductionist philosophies because consciousness, memory, and freedom are not reducible to spatially describable brain-states.

It resembles process philosophy because mind is becoming, not substance.

It resembles phenomenology because it begins from lived time and criticizes the objectification of experience.

It differs from most contemporary physicalism because it denies that mental life is exhausted by neural processes.

It differs from Cartesian dualism because the soul is not a static immaterial thing externally attached to a mechanical body. Soul and body interpenetrate in action, perception, and memory.

It differs from idealism because matter is real and not merely a representation in consciousness.

It differs from panpsychism because consciousness is tied primarily to life, choice, and indetermination, not simply attributed to all matter.

A contemporary Bergsonian philosophy of mind would likely need to revise his neuroscience while preserving his core insight: the lived, temporal, personal, action-oriented reality of mind is not captured by treating mental life as a set of spatially localizable states.

27. Principal difficulties and criticisms

The greatest difficulty is the relation between Bergson’s metaphysics and modern neuroscience. His claim that pure memories are not stored in the brain is difficult to reconcile with contemporary evidence about neural systems involved in memory. A defensible Bergsonian view today should not deny that brains are necessary for human remembering as we know it. Rather, it should argue that neural storage and retrieval do not exhaust the ontological and phenomenological reality of memory as lived duration.

A second difficulty is the causal relation between spirit and matter. Bergson wants to avoid both reductionism and an unintelligible interaction between separate substances. His temporal dualism helps, because spirit and matter are not two spatial things colliding. Still, the exact metaphysical mechanics of their relation remain difficult.

A third difficulty is the status of intuition. Bergson insists that intuition is methodical, but critics can ask how its results are checked. His best answer is that intuition must remain in dialogue with science, experience, and conceptual analysis. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy notes that Bergson intended dialogue with science, even though some of his biological speculations came to be regarded as outdated.

A fourth difficulty is theological. Bergson’s God is powerful philosophically but not easily aligned with all classical doctrines. God as creative love and “creator of creators” may look too dynamic for some traditional theologies and too theological for secular metaphysics. The interpretation must therefore remain precise: Bergson is not simply doing Catholic dogmatics, but neither is his theology detachable from his metaphysics of creative life.

A fifth difficulty concerns psychical research and survival. Bergson’s openness to telepathy and similar phenomena belongs to his filter theory of body and mind, but the evidential basis remains controversial. One can preserve the philosophical argument about consciousness overflowing cerebral action without accepting every empirical speculation Bergson entertained.

28. Non-English reception and interpretive debates

The non-English reception is especially helpful because it often refuses to separate the philosophy of mind from religion and metaphysics.

Recent Francophone work has re-centered the question of God, mysticism, and the meaning of the élan vital. A French review discussing work by Christiane Baka Okpobé notes that contemporary Bergson studies by figures such as Frédéric Worms, Ghislain Waterlot, François Azouvi, and Anthony Feneuil have reopened the question of God as the source of dynamic openness and of mysticism as the “reason” of the vital impulse.

Spanish-language scholarship likewise tends to treat mysticism as philosophically central rather than marginal. The UNED study mentioned above emphasizes Christian mysticism as a summit of Bergson’s account and as philosophically relevant to God, soul, and religion. A Mexican SciELO article, while engaging Deleuzian readings, stresses the link between duration, consciousness, intuition, and Christian mystical paths in Bergson’s own account.

Italian sources also emphasize that Matter and Memory remodels the body-spirit relation without falling into either idealism or physiological reductionism. Treccani presents the work as a transformation of the classic mind-body problem through perception and image, while the Italian DISF entry stresses Bergson’s open, non-dogmatic metaphysics in dialogue with empirical research.

In German discussion, Frédéric Worms has been presented as recovering Bergson’s account of memory against the idea that memory is merely storage in the brain, while also insisting that Bergson cannot simply be accepted uncritically today and must be modified in light of later developments.

This multilingual reception supports a key interpretive conclusion: the theological and mystical dimensions are not late decorative additions. They are one culmination of Bergson’s philosophy of mind.

29. Final synthesis

Bergson’s philosophy of mind begins with duration and ends with open creative love. Between those poles lies a remarkably coherent system.

At the psychological level, consciousness is qualitative duration.

At the perceptual level, the body selects images for possible action.

At the neurological level, the brain is a practical organ of attention, selection, and motor actualization.

At the mnemonic level, the past survives virtually and descends into the present according to need.

At the personal level, the self is the whole continuity of its lived past, capable of free acts when it expresses itself deeply.

At the biological level, consciousness marks life’s insertion of indetermination into matter.

At the cognitive level, intelligence spatializes, while intuition returns to duration.

At the religious level, the soul can pass from closed social pressure to open creative love.

At the theological level, God is the creative source of that love, not merely an object represented by doctrine.

So the Bergsonian philosophy of mind is not “mind inside brain,” nor “soul outside body,” nor “consciousness as representation.” It is a philosophy of living duration embodied for action. The brain is the hinge; memory is the depth; freedom is the creative act; intuition is the method; the soul is the whole person as spiritual duration; and mysticism is the highest intensification of consciousness into love.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
